[22] Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
When a body is dissected, its structures are cut apart in order to observe their physical attributes and their relationships to one another. Dissection is still used in medical schools, anatomy courses, and in pathology labs. In order to observe structures in living people, however, a number of imaging techniques have been developed.

[23] Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
The word "anatomy" comes from a Greek root that means "to cut apart." Human anatomy was first studied by observing the exterior of the body and observing the wounds of soldiers and other injuries. Later, physicians were allowed to dissect bodies of the dead to augment their knowledge. When a body is dissected, its structures are cut apart in order to observe their physical attributes and their relationships to one another. Dissection is still used in medical schools, anatomy courses, and in pathology labs. In order to observe structures in living people, however, a number of imaging techniques have been developed.

[24] Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
These techniques allow clinicians to visualize structures inside the living body such as a cancerous tumor or a fractured bone. Gross anatomy is the study of the larger structures of the body, those visible without the aid of magnification. Macro- means "large," thus, gross anatomy is also referred to as macroscopic anatomy. In contrast, micro- means "small," and microscopic anatomy is the study of structures that can be observed only with the use of a microscope or other magnification devices. Microscopic anatomy includes cytology, the study of cells and histology, the study of tissues. As the technology of microscopes has advanced, anatomists have been able to observe smaller and smaller structures of the body, from slices of large structures like the heart, to the three-dimensional structures of large molecules in the body. Anatomists take two general approaches to the study of the body's structures: regional and systemic. Regional anatomy is the study of the interrelationships of all of the structures in a specific body region, such as the abdomen. Studying regional anatomy helps us appreciate the interrelationships of body structures, such as how muscles, nerves, blood vessels, and other structures work together to serve a particular body region. In contrast, systemic anatomy is the study of the structures that make up a discrete body system—that is, a group of structures that work together to perform a unique body function. For example, a systemic anatomical study of the muscular system would consider all of the skeletal muscles of the body. Whereas anatomy is about structure, physiology is about function. Human physiology is the scientific study of the chemistry and physics of the structures of the body and the ways in which they work together to support the functions of life. Much of the study of physiology centers on the body's tendency toward homeostasis.

Regional anatomy is the study of the interrelationships of all of the structures in a specific body region, such as the abdomen. For example, a systemic anatomical study of the muscular system would consider all of the skeletal muscles of the body. Whereas anatomy is about structure, physiology is about function. Human physiology is the scientific study of the chemistry and physics of the structures of the body and the ways in which they work together to support the functions of life. These techniques allow clinicians to visualize structures inside the living body such as a cancerous tumor or a fractured bone. Like most scientific disciplines, anatomy has areas of specialization. Gross anatomy is the study of the larger structures of the body, those visible without the aid of magnification. Macro- means "large," thus, gross anatomy is also referred to as macroscopic anatomy. In contrast, micro- means "small," and microscopic anatomy is the study of structures that can be observed only with the use of a microscope or other magnification devices. Microscopic anatomy includes cytology, the study of cells and histology, the study of tissues. As the technology of microscopes has advanced, anatomists have been able to observe smaller and smaller structures of the body, from slices of large structures like the heart, to the three-dimensional structures of large molecules in the body. Anatomists take two general approaches to the study of the body's structures: regional and systemic. Regional anatomy is the study of the interrelationships of all of the structures in a specific body region, such as the abdomen. Studying regional anatomy helps us appreciate the interrelationships of body structures, such as how muscles, nerves, blood vessels, and other structures work together to serve a particular body region. In contrast, systemic anatomy is the study of the structures that make up a discrete body system—that is, a group of structures that work together to perform a unique body function. For example, a systemic anatomical study of the muscular system would consider all of the skeletal muscles of the body. Whereas anatomy is about structure, physiology is about function. Human physiology is the scientific study of the chemistry and physics of the structures of the body and the ways in which they work together to support the functions of life. Much of the study of physiology centers on the body's tendency toward homeostasis.

===

Homeostasis is the state of steady internal conditions maintained by living things. The study of physiology certainly includes observation, both with the naked eye and with microscopes, as well as manipulations and measurements.

Other movements are involuntary, meaning they are not under conscious control, such as the contraction of your pupil in bright light. Muscle tissue is classified into three types according to structure and function: skeletal, cardiac, and smooth. Skeletal muscle has long cylindrical fibers, is striated, and has many peripherally located nuclei. Skeletal muscle functions in voluntary movement, produces heat, and protects organs. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and around entrance points to body (e.g., mouth, anus). Cardiac muscle has short, branched fibers, is striated, and has a single central nucleus. Cardiac muscle contracts to pump blood and is located in the heart. Smooth muscle has short, spindle-shaped fibers, has no evident striation, and has a single nucleus in each fiber. Smooth muscle functions in involuntary movement, moves food, involuntary control of respiration, moves secretions, and regulates flow of blood in arteries by contraction. Smooth muscle is located in walls of major organs and passageways. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and its contraction makes possible locomotion, facial expressions, posture, and other voluntary movements of the body. Forty percent of your body mass is made up of skeletal muscle.

[22] Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
When a body is dissected, its structures are cut apart in order to observe their physical attributes and their relationships to one another. Dissection is still used in medical schools, anatomy courses, and in pathology labs. In order to observe structures in living people, however, a number of imaging techniques have been developed.

[23] Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
The word "anatomy" comes from a Greek root that means "to cut apart." Human anatomy was first studied by observing the exterior of the body and observing the wounds of soldiers and other injuries. Later, physicians were allowed to dissect bodies of the dead to augment their knowledge. When a body is dissected, its structures are cut apart in order to observe their physical attributes and their relationships to one another. Dissection is still used in medical schools, anatomy courses, and in pathology labs. In order to observe structures in living people, however, a number of imaging techniques have been developed.

[24] Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
These techniques allow clinicians to visualize structures inside the living body such as a cancerous tumor or a fractured bone. Gross anatomy is the study of the larger structures of the body, those visible without the aid of magnification. Macro- means "large," thus, gross anatomy is also referred to as macroscopic anatomy. In contrast, micro- means "small," and microscopic anatomy is the study of structures that can be observed only with the use of a microscope or other magnification devices. Microscopic anatomy includes cytology, the study of cells and histology, the study of tissues. As the technology of microscopes has advanced, anatomists have been able to observe smaller and smaller structures of the body, from slices of large structures like the heart, to the three-dimensional structures of large molecules in the body. Anatomists take two general approaches to the study of the body's structures: regional and systemic. Regional anatomy is the study of the interrelationships of all of the structures in a specific body region, such as the abdomen. Studying regional anatomy helps us appreciate the interrelationships of body structures, such as how muscles, nerves, blood vessels, and other structures work together to serve a particular body region. In contrast, systemic anatomy is the study of the structures that make up a discrete body system—that is, a group of structures that work together to perform a unique body function. For example, a systemic anatomical study of the muscular system would consider all of the skeletal muscles of the body. Whereas anatomy is about structure, physiology is about function. Human physiology is the scientific study of the chemistry and physics of the structures of the body and the ways in which they work together to support the functions of life. Much of the study of physiology centers on the body's tendency toward homeostasis.

Muscle tissue is classified into three types according to structure and function: skeletal, cardiac, and smooth. Skeletal muscle has long cylindrical fibers, is striated, and has many peripherally located nuclei. Skeletal muscle functions in voluntary movement, produces heat, and protects organs. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and around entrance points to body (e.g., mouth, anus). Cardiac muscle has short, branched fibers, is striated, and has a single central nucleus. Cardiac muscle contracts to pump blood and is located in the heart. Smooth muscle has short, spindle-shaped fibers, has no evident striation, and has a single nucleus in each fiber. Smooth muscle functions in involuntary movement, moves food, involuntary control of respiration, moves secretions, and regulates flow of blood in arteries by contraction. Smooth muscle is located in walls of major organs and passageways. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and its contraction makes possible locomotion, facial expressions, posture, and other voluntary movements of the body. Forty percent of your body mass is made up of skeletal muscle.

[3] (p.163) The Tissue Level of Organization > Muscle Tissue and Motion

Other movements are involuntary, meaning they are not under conscious control, such as the contraction of your pupil in bright light. Muscle tissue is classified into three types according to structure and function: skeletal, cardiac, and smooth. Skeletal muscle has long cylindrical fibers, is striated, and has many peripherally located nuclei. Skeletal muscle functions in voluntary movement, produces heat, and protects organs. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and around entrance points to body (e.g., mouth, anus). Cardiac muscle has short, branched fibers, is striated, and has a single central nucleus. Cardiac muscle contracts to pump blood and is located in the heart. Smooth muscle has short, spindle-shaped fibers, has no evident striation, and has a single nucleus in each fiber. Smooth muscle functions in involuntary movement, moves food, involuntary control of respiration, moves secretions, and regulates flow of blood in arteries by contraction. Smooth muscle is located in walls of major organs and passageways. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and its contraction makes possible locomotion, facial expressions, posture, and other voluntary movements of the body. Forty percent of your body mass is made up of skeletal muscle.

[23] (p.24) An Introduction to the Human Body > Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
---
The word "anatomy" comes from a Greek root that means "to cut apart." Human anatomy was first studied by observing the exterior of the body and observing the wounds of soldiers and other injuries. Later, physicians were allowed to dissect bodies of the dead to augment their knowledge. When a body is dissected, its structures are cut apart in order to observe their physical attributes and their relationships to one another. Dissection is still used in medical schools, anatomy courses, and in pathology labs. In order to observe structures in living people, however, a number of imaging techniques have been developed.

[24] (p.24, score=0.99) An Introduction to the Human Body > Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
---
These techniques allow clinicians to visualize structures inside the living body such as a cancerous tumor or a fractured bone. Gross anatomy is the study of the larger structures of the body, those visible without the aid of magnification. Macro- means "large," thus, gross anatomy is also referred to as macroscopic anatomy. In contrast, micro- means "small," and microscopic anatomy is the study of structures that can be observed only with the use of a microscope or other magnification devices. Microscopic anatomy includes cytology, the study of cells and histology, the study of tissues. As the technology of microscopes has advanced, anatomists have been able to observe smaller and smaller structures of the body, from slices of large structures like the heart, to the three-dimensional structures of large molecules in the body. Anatomists take two general approaches to the study of the body's structures: regional and systemic. Regional anatomy is the study of the interrelationships of all of the structures in a specific body region, such as the abdomen. Studying regional anatomy helps us appreciate the interrelationships of body structures, such as how muscles, nerves, blood vessels, and other structures work together to serve a particular body region. In contrast, systemic anatomy is the study of the structures that make up a discrete body system—that is, a group of structures that work together to perform a unique body function. For example, a systemic anatomical study of the muscular system would consider all of the skeletal muscles of the body. Whereas anatomy is about structure, physiology is about function. Human physiology is the scientific study of the chemistry and physics of the structures of the body and the ways in which they work together to support the functions of life. Much of the study of physiology centers on the body's tendency toward homeostasis.

a Socratic anatomy tutor.

Hard rules:

Textbook propositions:
[1] Muscle Tissue and Motion
They are contractile, meaning they can shorten and generate a pulling force. When attached between two movable objects, in other words, bones, contractions of the muscles cause the bones to move. Some muscle movement is voluntary, which means it is under conscious control.

[2] Muscle Tissue and Motion
Cancellous bone looks like a sponge under the microscope and contains empty spaces between trabeculae, or arches of bone proper. It is lighter than compact bone and found in the interior of some bones and at the end of long bones. Muscles are contractile, meaning they can shorten and generate a pulling force. When attached between two movable objects, in other words, bones, contractions of the muscles cause the bones to move. Some muscle movement is voluntary, which means it is under conscious control.

[3] Muscle Tissue and Motion
Other movements are involuntary, meaning they are not under conscious control, such as the contraction of your pupil in bright light. Muscle tissue is classified into three types according to structure and function: skeletal, cardiac, and smooth. Skeletal muscle has long cylindrical fibers, is striated, and has many peripherally located nuclei. Skeletal muscle functions in voluntary movement, produces heat, and protects organs. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and around entrance points to body (e.g., mouth, anus). Cardiac muscle has short, branched fibers, is striated, and has a single central nucleus. Cardiac muscle contracts to pump blood and is located in the heart. Smooth muscle has short, spindle-shaped fibers, has no evident striation, and has a single nucleus in each fiber. Smooth muscle functions in involuntary movement, moves food, involuntary control of respiration, moves secretions, and regulates flow of blood in arteries by contraction. Smooth muscle is located in walls of major organs and passageways. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and its contraction makes possible locomotion, facial expressions, posture, and other voluntary movements of the body. Forty percent of your body mass is made up of skeletal muscle.

Shivering is an involuntary contraction of skeletal muscles in response to perceived lower than normal body temperature. [22] Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
When a body is dissected, its structures are cut apart in order to observe their physical attributes and their relationships to one another. Dissection is still used in medical schools, anatomy courses, and in pathology labs. In order to observe structures in living people, however, a number of imaging techniques have been developed.

[23] Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
The word "anatomy" comes from a Greek root that means "to cut apart." Human anatomy was first studied by observing the exterior of the body and observing the wounds of soldiers and other injuries. Later, physicians were allowed to dissect bodies of the dead to augment their knowledge. When a body is dissected, its structures are cut apart in order to observe their physical attributes and their relationships to one another. Dissection is still used in medical schools, anatomy courses, and in pathology labs. In order to observe structures in living people, however, a number of imaging techniques have been developed.

[24] Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
These techniques allow clinicians to visualize structures inside the living body such as a cancerous tumor or a fractured bone. Gross anatomy is the study of the larger structures of the body, those visible without the aid of magnification. Macro- means "large," thus, gross anatomy is also referred to as macroscopic anatomy. In contrast, micro- means "small," and microscopic anatomy is the study of structures that can be observed only with the use of a microscope or other magnification devices. Microscopic anatomy includes cytology, the study of cells and histology, the study of tissues. As the technology of microscopes has advanced, anatomists have been able to observe smaller and smaller structures of the body, from slices of large structures like the heart, to the three-dimensional structures of large molecules in the body. Anatomists take two general approaches to the study of the body's structures: regional and systemic. Regional anatomy is the study of the interrelationships of all of the structures in a specific body region, such as the abdomen. Studying regional anatomy helps us appreciate the interrelationships of body structures, such as how muscles, nerves, blood vessels, and other structures work together to serve a particular body region. In contrast, systemic anatomy is the study of the structures that make up a discrete body system—that is, a group of structures that work together to perform a unique body function. For example, a systemic anatomical study of the muscular system would consider all of the skeletal muscles of the body. Whereas anatomy is about structure, physiology is about function. Human physiology is the scientific study of the chemistry and physics of the structures of the body and the ways in which they work together to support the functions of life. Much of the study of physiology centers on the body's tendency toward homeostasis.

[3] (p.163) The Tissue Level of Organization > Muscle Tissue and Motion
---
Other movements are involuntary, meaning they are not under conscious control, such as the contraction of your pupil in bright light. Muscle tissue is classified into three types according to structure and function: skeletal, cardiac, and smooth. Skeletal muscle has long cylindrical fibers, is striated, and has many peripherally located nuclei. Skeletal muscle functions in voluntary movement, produces heat, and protects organs. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and around entrance points to body (e.g., mouth, anus). Cardiac muscle has short, branched fibers, is striated, and has a single central nucleus. Cardiac muscle contracts to pump blood and is located in the heart. Smooth muscle has short, spindle-shaped fibers, has no evident striation, and has a single nucleus in each fiber. Smooth muscle functions in involuntary movement, moves food, involuntary control of respiration, moves secretions, and regulates flow of blood in arteries by contraction. Smooth muscle is located in walls of major organs and passageways. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and its contraction makes possible locomotion, facial expressions, posture, and other voluntary movements of the body. Forty percent of your body mass is made up of skeletal muscle.

Cardiomyocyte attach to one another with specialized cell junctions called intercalated discs.

When a body is dissected, its structures are cut apart in order to observe their physical attributes and their relationships to one another. Dissection is still used in medical schools, anatomy courses, and in pathology labs. In order to observe structures in living people, however, a number of imaging techniques have been developed.

[23] (p.24) An Introduction to the Human Body > Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
---
The word "anatomy" comes from a Greek root that means "to cut apart." Human anatomy was first studied by observing the exterior of the body and observing the wounds of soldiers and other injuries. Later, physicians were allowed to dissect bodies of the dead to augment their knowledge. When a body is dissected, its structures are cut apart in order to observe their physical attributes and their relationships to one another. Dissection is still used in medical schools, anatomy courses, and in pathology labs. In order to observe structures in living people, however, a number of imaging techniques have been developed.

===

[24] (p.24, score=0.99) An Introduction to the Human Body > Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
---
These techniques allow clinicians to visualize structures inside the living body such as a cancerous tumor or a fractured bone. Gross anatomy is the study of the larger structures of the body, those visible without the aid of magnification. Macro- means "large," thus, gross anatomy is also referred to as macroscopic anatomy. In contrast, micro- means "small," and microscopic anatomy is the study of structures that can be observed only with the use of a microscope or other magnification devices. Microscopic anatomy includes cytology, the study of cells and histology, the study of tissues. As the technology of microscopes has advanced, anatomists have been able to observe smaller and smaller structures of the body, from slices of large structures like the heart, to the three-dimensional structures of large molecules in the body. Anatomists take two general approaches to the study of the body's structures: regional and systemic. Regional anatomy is the study of the interrelationships of all of the structures in a specific body region, such as the abdomen. Studying regional anatomy helps us appreciate the interrelationships of body structures, such as how muscles, nerves, blood vessels, and other structures work together to serve a particular body region. In contrast, systemic anatomy is the study of the structures that make up a discrete body system—that is, a group of structures that work together to perform a unique body function. For example, a systemic anatomical study of the muscular system would consider all of the skeletal muscles of the body. Whereas anatomy is about structure, physiology is about function. Human physiology is the scientific study of the chemistry and physics of the structures of the body and the ways in which they work together to support the functions of life. Much of the study of physiology centers on the body's tendency toward homeostasis.

===

Cancellous bone looks like a sponge under the microscope and contains empty spaces between trabeculae, or arches of bone proper. It is lighter than compact bone and found in the interior of some bones and at the end of long bones. Muscles are contractile, meaning they can shorten and generate a pulling force. [16] (p.163) The Tissue Level of Organization > Muscle Tissue and Motion
---
Other movements are involuntary, meaning they are not under conscious control, such as the contraction of your pupil in bright light. Muscle tissue is classified into three types according to structure and function: skeletal, cardiac, and smooth. Skeletal muscle has long cylindrical fibers, is striated, and has many peripherally located nuclei. Skeletal muscle functions in voluntary movement, produces heat, and protects organs. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and around entrance points to body (e.g., mouth, anus). Cardiac muscle has short, branched fibers, is striated, and has a single central nucleus. Cardiac muscle contracts to pump blood and is located in the heart. Smooth muscle has short, spindle-shaped fibers, has no evident striation, and has a single nucleus in each fiber. Smooth muscle functions in involuntary movement, moves food, involuntary control of respiration, moves secretions, and regulates flow of blood in arteries by contraction. Smooth muscle is located in walls of major organs and passageways. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and its contraction makes possible locomotion, facial expressions, posture, and other voluntary movements of the body. Forty percent of your body mass is made up of skeletal muscle.

[22] (p.24) An Introduction to the Human Body > Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
---
When a body is dissected, its structures are cut apart in order to observe their physical attributes and their relationships to one another. Dissection is still used in medical schools, anatomy courses, and in pathology labs. In order to observe structures in living people, however, a number of imaging techniques have been developed.

===

[23] (p.24) An Introduction to the Human Body > Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
---
The word "anatomy" comes from a Greek root that means "to cut apart." Human anatomy was first studied by observing the exterior of the body and observing the wounds of soldiers and other injuries. Later, physicians were allowed to dissect bodies of the dead to augment their knowledge. When a body is dissected, its structures are cut apart in order to observe their physical attributes and their relationships to one another. Dissection is still used in medical schools, anatomy courses, and in pathology labs. In order to observe structures in living people, however, a number of imaging techniques have been developed.

===

[24] (p.24, score=0.99) An Introduction to the Human Body > Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
---
These techniques allow clinicians to visualize structures inside the living body such as a cancerous tumor or a fractured bone. Gross anatomy is the study of the larger structures of the body, those visible without the aid of magnification. Macro- means "large," thus, gross anatomy is also referred to as macroscopic anatomy. In contrast, micro- means "small," and microscopic anatomy is the study of structures that can be observed only with the use of a microscope or other magnification devices. Microscopic anatomy includes cytology, the study of cells and histology, the study of tissues. As the technology of microscopes has advanced, anatomists have been able to observe smaller and smaller structures of the body, from slices of large structures like the heart, to the three-dimensional structures of large molecules in the body. Anatomists take two general approaches to the study of the body's structures: regional and systemic. Regional anatomy is the study of the interrelationships of all of the structures in a specific body region, such as the abdomen. Studying regional anatomy helps us appreciate the interrelationships of body structures, such as how muscles, nerves, blood vessels, and other structures work together to serve a particular body region. In contrast, systemic anatomy is the study of the structures that make up a discrete body system—that is, a group of structures that work together to perform a unique body function. For example, a systemic anatomical study of the muscular system would consider all of the skeletal muscles of the body. Whereas anatomy is about structure, physiology is about function. Human physiology is the scientific study of the chemistry and physics of the structures of the body and the ways in which they work together to support the functions of life. Much of the study of physiology centers on the body's tendency toward homeostasis.

===

- Never state "you're correct" unless you also ask for reasoning or application.
- Keep output to 2-4 sentences.
- End with exactly one question.
- Ask only one question total.
- If the student is incorrect, do not bluntly correct; use probing guidance.
- Do NOT use strong affirmation phrases (for example: "Exactly", "Correct", "That's right", "You got it")
  unless student_state is exactly "correct".

Behavior by student_state:
- correct: affirm briefly, then deepen via mechanism/function.
- partial_correct: acknowledge only the accurate fragment (use "close"/"partly right"), then probe missing link.
- incorrect: do not affirm; probe reasoning path and guide toward self-correction.
- question: briefly answer clarification, then return to core reasoning.
- irrelevant: redirect to current human anatomy target.
- low_effort: request a concrete attempt before moving on.

Hint-level behavior:
- level 1: broad activation question.
- level 2: narrower mechanism/relationship question.
- level 3: direct but non-revealing final push.

Internal quality check before finalizing (EULER-lite):
- Question present: ends with exactly one real question.
- Relevance: tied to latest student message and target concept.
- Helpful: advances reasoning, not generic filler.
- No reveal: locked answer/synonym is not inferable in one hop.
- Avoid repetitive empathy wrappers and filler phrases.

Banned lead-ins — NEVER start a sentence with these:
- "I can see"
- "I notice"
- "I hear"
- "That's okay"
- "I understand"
- "I see that"
- "It sounds like"
Violation of any banned lead-in = automatic rewrite.

Output contract:
- At most 4 sentences total.
- Sentence 1: brief targeted acknowledgment (optional).
- Sentence 2-3: focused reasoning scaffold.
- Final sentence: exactly one question.
Output only the final tutor message.

[1] Muscle Tissue and Motion
They are contractile, meaning they can shorten and generate a pulling force. When attached between two movable objects, in other words, bones, contractions of the muscles cause the bones to move. Some muscle movement is voluntary, which means it is under conscious control.

[2] Muscle Tissue and Motion
Cancellous bone looks like a sponge under the microscope and contains empty spaces between trabeculae, or arches of bone proper. It is lighter than compact bone and found in the interior of some bones and at the end of long bones. Muscles are contractile, meaning they can shorten and generate a pulling force. When attached between two movable objects, in other words, bones, contractions of the muscles cause the bones to move. Some muscle movement is voluntary, which means it is under conscious control.

[3] Muscle Tissue and Motion
Other movements are involuntary, meaning they are not under conscious control, such as the contraction of your pupil in bright light. Muscle tissue is classified into three types according to structure and function: skeletal, cardiac, and smooth. Skeletal muscle has long cylindrical fibers, is striated, and has many peripherally located nuclei. Skeletal muscle functions in voluntary movement, produces heat, and protects organs. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and around entrance points to body (e.g., mouth, anus). Cardiac muscle has short, branched fibers, is striated, and has a single central nucleus. Cardiac muscle contracts to pump blood and is located in the heart. Smooth muscle has short, spindle-shaped fibers, has no evident striation, and has a single nucleus in each fiber. Smooth muscle functions in involuntary movement, moves food, involuntary control of respiration, moves secretions, and regulates flow of blood in arteries by contraction. Smooth muscle is located in walls of major organs and passageways. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and its contraction makes possible locomotion, facial expressions, posture, and other voluntary movements of the body. Forty percent of your body mass is made up of skeletal muscle.

[22] Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
When a body is dissected, its structures are cut apart in order to observe their physical attributes and their relationships to one another. Dissection is still used in medical schools, anatomy courses, and in pathology labs. In order to observe structures in living people, however, a number of imaging techniques have been developed.

[23] Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
The word "anatomy" comes from a Greek root that means "to cut apart." Human anatomy was first studied by observing the exterior of the body and observing the wounds of soldiers and other injuries. Later, physicians were allowed to dissect bodies of the dead to augment their knowledge. When a body is dissected, its structures are cut apart in order to observe their physical attributes and their relationships to one another. Dissection is still used in medical schools, anatomy courses, and in pathology labs. In order to observe structures in living people, however, a number of imaging techniques have been developed.

[24] Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
These techniques allow clinicians to visualize structures inside the living body such as a cancerous tumor or a fractured bone. Gross anatomy is the study of the larger structures of the body, those visible without the aid of magnification. Macro- means "large," thus, gross anatomy is also referred to as macroscopic anatomy. In contrast, micro- means "small," and microscopic anatomy is the study of structures that can be observed only with the use of a microscope or other magnification devices. Microscopic anatomy includes cytology, the study of cells and histology, the study of tissues. As the technology of microscopes has advanced, anatomists have been able to observe smaller and smaller structures of the body, from slices of large structures like the heart, to the three-dimensional structures of large molecules in the body. Anatomists take two general approaches to the study of the body's structures: regional and systemic. Regional anatomy is the study of the interrelationships of all of the structures in a specific body region, such as the abdomen. Studying regional anatomy helps us appreciate the interrelationships of body structures, such as how muscles, nerves, blood vessels, and other structures work together to serve a particular body region. In contrast, systemic anatomy is the study of the structures that make up a discrete body system—that is, a group of structures that work together to perform a unique body function. For example, a systemic anatomical study of the muscular system would consider all of the skeletal muscles of the body. Whereas anatomy is about structure, physiology is about function. Human physiology is the scientific study of the chemistry and physics of the structures of the body and the ways in which they work together to support the functions of life. Much of the study of physiology centers on the body's tendency toward homeostasis.

Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and around entrance points to body (e.g., mouth, anus). Cardiac muscle has short, branched fibers, is striated, and has a single central nucleus. Cardiac muscle contracts to pump blood and is located in the heart. Smooth muscle has short, spindle-shaped fibers, has no evident striation, and has a single nucleus in each fiber. Smooth muscle functions in involuntary movement, moves food, involuntary control of respiration, moves secretions, and regulates flow of blood in arteries by contraction. Smooth muscle is located in walls of major organs and passageways. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and its contraction makes possible locomotion, facial expressions, posture, and other voluntary movements of the body. Forty percent of your body mass is made up of skeletal muscle.

These techniques allow clinicians to visualize structures inside the living body such as a cancerous tumor or a fractured bone. Gross anatomy is the study of the larger structures of the body, those visible without the aid of magnification. Macro- means "large," thus, gross anatomy is also referred to as macroscopic anatomy. In contrast, micro- means "small," and microscopic anatomy is the study of structures that can be observed only with the use of a microscope or other magnification devices. Microscopic anatomy includes cytology, the study of cells and histology, the study of tissues. As the technology of microscopes has advanced, anatomists have been able to observe smaller and smaller structures of the body, from slices of large structures like the heart, to the three-dimensional structures of large molecules in the body. Anatomists take two general approaches to the study of the body's structures: regional and systemic. Regional anatomy is the study of the interrelationships of all of the structures in a specific body region, such as the abdomen. Studying regional anatomy helps us appreciate the interrelationships of body structures, such as how muscles, nerves, blood vessels, and other structures work together to serve a particular body region. In contrast, systemic anatomy is the study of the structures that make up a discrete body system—that is, a group of structures that work together to perform a unique body function. For example, a systemic anatomical study of the muscular system would consider all of the skeletal muscles of the body. Whereas anatomy is about structure, physiology is about function. Human physiology is the scientific study of the chemistry and physics of the structures of the body and the ways in which they work together to support the functions of life. Much of the study of physiology centers on the body's tendency toward homeostasis.

Cardiomyocyte attach to one another with specialized cell junctions called intercalated discs.

Studying regional anatomy helps us appreciate the interrelationships of body structures, such as how muscles, nerves, blood vessels, and other structures work together to serve a particular body region. In contrast, systemic anatomy is the study of the structures that make up a discrete body system—that is, a group of structures that work together to perform a unique body function. For example, a systemic anatomical study of the muscular system would consider all of the skeletal muscles of the body. Whereas anatomy is about structure, physiology is about function. Human physiology is the scientific study of the chemistry and physics of the structures of the body and the ways in which they work together to support the functions of life. Much of the study of physiology centers on the body's tendency toward homeostasis.

===

[26] (p.24) An Introduction to the Human Body > Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
---
Homeostasis is the state of steady internal conditions maintained by living things. The study of physiology certainly includes observation, both with the naked eye and with microscopes, as well as manipulations and measurements.

a Socratic anatomy tutor.

- partial_correct: acknowledge only the accurate fragment (use "close"/"partly right"), then probe missing link.
- incorrect: do not affirm; probe reasoning path and guide toward self-correction.
- question: briefly answer clarification, then return to core reasoning.
- irrelevant: redirect to current human anatomy target.
- low_effort: request a concrete attempt before moving on.

Hint-level behavior:
- level 1: broad activation question.
- level 2: narrower mechanism/relationship question.
- level 3: direct but non-revealing final push.

Internal quality check before finalizing (EULER-lite):
- Question present: ends with exactly one real question.
- Relevance: tied to latest student message and target concept.
- Helpful: advances reasoning, not generic filler.
- No reveal: locked answer/synonym is not inferable in one hop.

Banned lead-ins — NEVER start a sentence with these:
- "I can see"
- "I notice"
- "I hear"
- "That's okay"
- "I understand"
- "I see that"
- "It sounds like"

Textbook propositions:
[1] Muscle Tissue and Motion
They are contractile, meaning they can shorten and generate a pulling force. When attached between two movable objects, in other words, bones, contractions of the muscles cause the bones to move. Some muscle movement is voluntary, which means it is under conscious control.

[2] Muscle Tissue and Motion
Cancellous bone looks like a sponge under the microscope and contains empty spaces between trabeculae, or arches of bone proper. It is lighter than compact bone and found in the interior of some bones and at the end of long bones. Muscles are contractile, meaning they can shorten and generate a pulling force. When attached between two movable objects, in other words, bones, contractions of the muscles cause the bones to move. Some muscle movement is voluntary, which means it is under conscious control.

[3] Muscle Tissue and Motion
Other movements are involuntary, meaning they are not under conscious control, such as the contraction of your pupil in bright light. Muscle tissue is classified into three types according to structure and function: skeletal, cardiac, and smooth. Skeletal muscle has long cylindrical fibers, is striated, and has many peripherally located nuclei. Skeletal muscle functions in voluntary movement, produces heat, and protects organs. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and around entrance points to body (e.g., mouth, anus). Cardiac muscle has short, branched fibers, is striated, and has a single central nucleus. Cardiac muscle contracts to pump blood and is located in the heart. Smooth muscle has short, spindle-shaped fibers, has no evident striation, and has a single nucleus in each fiber. Smooth muscle functions in involuntary movement, moves food, involuntary control of respiration, moves secretions, and regulates flow of blood in arteries by contraction. Smooth muscle is located in walls of major organs and passageways. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and its contraction makes possible locomotion, facial expressions, posture, and other voluntary movements of the body. Forty percent of your body mass is made up of skeletal muscle.

[4] Muscle Tissue and Motion
Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and its contraction makes possible locomotion, facial expressions, posture, and other voluntary movements of the body. Forty percent of your body mass is made up of skeletal muscle.

[5] Muscle Tissue and Motion
Skeletal muscles generate heat as a byproduct of their contraction and thus participate in thermal homeostasis. Shivering is an involuntary contraction of skeletal muscles in response to perceived lower than normal body temperature. The muscle cell, or myocyte, develops from myoblasts derived from the mesoderm.

[6] Muscle Tissue and Motion
Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and its contraction makes possible locomotion, facial expressions, posture, and other voluntary movements of the body. Forty percent of your body mass is made up of skeletal muscle. Skeletal muscles generate heat as a byproduct of their contraction and thus participate in thermal homeostasis. Shivering is an involuntary contraction of skeletal muscles in response to perceived lower than normal body temperature. The muscle cell, or myocyte, develops from myoblasts derived from the mesoderm.

[7] Muscle Tissue and Motion
Myocytes and their numbers remain relatively constant throughout life. Skeletal muscle tissue is arranged in bundles surrounded by connective tissue. Under the light microscope, muscle cells appear striated with many nuclei squeezed along the membranes. The striation is due to the regular alternation of the contractile proteins actin and myosin, along with the structural proteins that couple the contractile proteins to connective tissues. The cells are multinucleated as a result of the fusion of the many myoblasts that fuse to form each long muscle fiber. Cardiac muscle forms the contractile walls of the heart. The cells of cardiac muscle, known as cardiomyocytes, also appear striated under the microscope. Unlike skeletal muscle fibers, cardiomyocytes are single cells typically with a single centrally located nucleus. A principal characteristic of cardiomyocytes is that they contract on their own intrinsic rhythms without any external stimulation. Cardiomyocyte attach to one another with specialized cell junctions called intercalated discs.

[8] Muscle Tissue and Motion
Shivering is an involuntary contraction of skeletal muscles in response to perceived lower than normal body temperature. The muscle cell, or myocyte, develops from myoblasts derived from the mesoderm. Myocytes and their numbers remain relatively constant throughout life. [22] Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
When a body is dissected, its structures are cut apart in order to observe their physical attributes and their relationships to one another. Dissection is still used in medical schools, anatomy courses, and in pathology labs. In order to observe structures in living people, however, a number of imaging techniques have been developed.

[23] Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
The word "anatomy" comes from a Greek root that means "to cut apart." Human anatomy was first studied by observing the exterior of the body and observing the wounds of soldiers and other injuries. Later, physicians were allowed to dissect bodies of the dead to augment their knowledge. When a body is dissected, its structures are cut apart in order to observe their physical attributes and their relationships to one another. Dissection is still used in medical schools, anatomy courses, and in pathology labs. In order to observe structures in living people, however, a number of imaging techniques have been developed.

[24] Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
These techniques allow clinicians to visualize structures inside the living body such as a cancerous tumor or a fractured bone. Gross anatomy is the study of the larger structures of the body, those visible without the aid of magnification. Macro- means "large," thus, gross anatomy is also referred to as macroscopic anatomy. In contrast, micro- means "small," and microscopic anatomy is the study of structures that can be observed only with the use of a microscope or other magnification devices. Microscopic anatomy includes cytology, the study of cells and histology, the study of tissues. As the technology of microscopes has advanced, anatomists have been able to observe smaller and smaller structures of the body, from slices of large structures like the heart, to the three-dimensional structures of large molecules in the body. Anatomists take two general approaches to the study of the body's structures: regional and systemic. Regional anatomy is the study of the interrelationships of all of the structures in a specific body region, such as the abdomen. Studying regional anatomy helps us appreciate the interrelationships of body structures, such as how muscles, nerves, blood vessels, and other structures work together to serve a particular body region. In contrast, systemic anatomy is the study of the structures that make up a discrete body system—that is, a group of structures that work together to perform a unique body function. For example, a systemic anatomical study of the muscular system would consider all of the skeletal muscles of the body. Whereas anatomy is about structure, physiology is about function. Human physiology is the scientific study of the chemistry and physics of the structures of the body and the ways in which they work together to support the functions of life. Much of the study of physiology centers on the body's tendency toward homeostasis.

Muscle tissue is classified into three types according to structure and function: skeletal, cardiac, and smooth. Skeletal muscle has long cylindrical fibers, is striated, and has many peripherally located nuclei. Skeletal muscle functions in voluntary movement, produces heat, and protects organs. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and around entrance points to body (e.g., mouth, anus). Cardiac muscle has short, branched fibers, is striated, and has a single central nucleus. Cardiac muscle contracts to pump blood and is located in the heart. Smooth muscle has short, spindle-shaped fibers, has no evident striation, and has a single nucleus in each fiber. Smooth muscle functions in involuntary movement, moves food, involuntary control of respiration, moves secretions, and regulates flow of blood in arteries by contraction. Smooth muscle is located in walls of major organs and passageways. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and its contraction makes possible locomotion, facial expressions, posture, and other voluntary movements of the body. Forty percent of your body mass is made up of skeletal muscle.

You are
a Socratic tutor in human anatomy working with a student.

Global rules for all teacher wrappers:
- Keep language academically focused and concise.

a Socratic anatomy tutor.
The student is being evaluated on human anatomy reasoning quality.

Hard rules:
- Never reveal the answer directly or indirectly.
- Never state "you're correct" unless you also ask for reasoning or application.
- Keep output to 2-4 sentences.
- End with exactly one question.
- Ask only one question total.
- If the student is incorrect, do not bluntly correct; use probing guidance.
- Do NOT use strong affirmation phrases (for example: "Exactly", "Correct", "That's right", "You got it")
  unless student_state is exactly "correct".

Behavior by student_state:
- correct: affirm briefly, then deepen via mechanism/function.
- partial_correct: acknowledge only the accurate fragment (use "close"/"partly right"), then probe missing link.
- incorrect: do not affirm; probe reasoning path and guide toward self-correction.
- question: briefly answer clarification, then return to core reasoning.
- irrelevant: redirect to current human anatomy target.
- low_effort: request a concrete attempt before moving on.

Hint-level behavior:
- level 1: broad activation question.
- level 2: narrower mechanism/relationship question.
- level 3: direct but non-revealing final push.

Internal quality check before finalizing (EULER-lite):
- Question present: ends with exactly one real question.
- Relevance: tied to latest student message and target concept.
- Helpful: advances reasoning, not generic filler.
- No reveal: locked answer/synonym is not inferable in one hop.
- Avoid repetitive empathy wrappers and filler phrases.

Banned lead-ins — NEVER start a sentence with these:
- "I can see"
- "I notice"
- "I hear"
- "That's okay"
- "I understand"
- "I see that"
- "It sounds like"
Violation of any banned lead-in = automatic rewrite.

Output contract:
- At most 4 sentences total.
- Sentence 1: brief targeted acknowledgment (optional).
- Sentence 2-3: focused reasoning scaffold.
- Final sentence: exactly one question.
Output only the final tutor message.


Textbook propositions:
[1] Muscle Tissue and Motion
They are contractile, meaning they can shorten and generate a pulling force. When attached between two movable objects, in other words, bones, contractions of the muscles cause the bones to move. Some muscle movement is voluntary, which means it is under conscious control.

[2] Muscle Tissue and Motion
Cancellous bone looks like a sponge under the microscope and contains empty spaces between trabeculae, or arches of bone proper. It is lighter than compact bone and found in the interior of some bones and at the end of long bones. Muscles are contractile, meaning they can shorten and generate a pulling force. When attached between two movable objects, in other words, bones, contractions of the muscles cause the bones to move. Some muscle movement is voluntary, which means it is under conscious control.

[3] Muscle Tissue and Motion
Other movements are involuntary, meaning they are not under conscious control, such as the contraction of your pupil in bright light. Muscle tissue is classified into three types according to structure and function: skeletal, cardiac, and smooth. Skeletal muscle has long cylindrical fibers, is striated, and has many peripherally located nuclei. Skeletal muscle functions in voluntary movement, produces heat, and protects organs. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and around entrance points to body (e.g., mouth, anus). Cardiac muscle has short, branched fibers, is striated, and has a single central nucleus. Cardiac muscle contracts to pump blood and is located in the heart. Smooth muscle has short, spindle-shaped fibers, has no evident striation, and has a single nucleus in each fiber. Smooth muscle functions in involuntary movement, moves food, involuntary control of respiration, moves secretions, and regulates flow of blood in arteries by contraction. Smooth muscle is located in walls of major organs and passageways. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and its contraction makes possible locomotion, facial expressions, posture, and other voluntary movements of the body. Forty percent of your body mass is made up of skeletal muscle.

[4] Muscle Tissue and Motion
Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and its contraction makes possible locomotion, facial expressions, posture, and other voluntary movements of the body. Forty percent of your body mass is made up of skeletal muscle.

[5] Muscle Tissue and Motion
Skeletal muscles generate heat as a byproduct of their contraction and thus participate in thermal homeostasis. Shivering is an involuntary contraction of skeletal muscles in response to perceived lower than normal body temperature. The muscle cell, or myocyte, develops from myoblasts derived from the mesoderm.

[6] Muscle Tissue and Motion

[22] Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
When a body is dissected, its structures are cut apart in order to observe their physical attributes and their relationships to one another. Dissection is still used in medical schools, anatomy courses, and in pathology labs. In order to observe structures in living people, however, a number of imaging techniques have been developed.

[23] Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
The word "anatomy" comes from a Greek root that means "to cut apart." Human anatomy was first studied by observing the exterior of the body and observing the wounds of soldiers and other injuries. Later, physicians were allowed to dissect bodies of the dead to augment their knowledge. When a body is dissected, its structures are cut apart in order to observe their physical attributes and their relationships to one another. Dissection is still used in medical schools, anatomy courses, and in pathology labs. In order to observe structures in living people, however, a number of imaging techniques have been developed.

[24] Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
These techniques allow clinicians to visualize structures inside the living body such as a cancerous tumor or a fractured bone. Gross anatomy is the study of the larger structures of the body, those visible without the aid of magnification. Macro- means "large," thus, gross anatomy is also referred to as macroscopic anatomy. In contrast, micro- means "small," and microscopic anatomy is the study of structures that can be observed only with the use of a microscope or other magnification devices. Microscopic anatomy includes cytology, the study of cells and histology, the study of tissues. As the technology of microscopes has advanced, anatomists have been able to observe smaller and smaller structures of the body, from slices of large structures like the heart, to the three-dimensional structures of large molecules in the body. Anatomists take two general approaches to the study of the body's structures: regional and systemic. Regional anatomy is the study of the interrelationships of all of the structures in a specific body region, such as the abdomen. Studying regional anatomy helps us appreciate the interrelationships of body structures, such as how muscles, nerves, blood vessels, and other structures work together to serve a particular body region. In contrast, systemic anatomy is the study of the structures that make up a discrete body system—that is, a group of structures that work together to perform a unique body function. For example, a systemic anatomical study of the muscular system would consider all of the skeletal muscles of the body. Whereas anatomy is about structure, physiology is about function. Human physiology is the scientific study of the chemistry and physics of the structures of the body and the ways in which they work together to support the functions of life. Much of the study of physiology centers on the body's tendency toward homeostasis.

Gross anatomy is the study of the larger structures of the body, those visible without the aid of magnification. Macro- means "large," thus, gross anatomy is also referred to as macroscopic anatomy. In contrast, micro- means "small," and microscopic anatomy is the study of structures that can be observed only with the use of a microscope or other magnification devices. Microscopic anatomy includes cytology, the study of cells and histology, the study of tissues. As the technology of microscopes has advanced, anatomists have been able to observe smaller and smaller structures of the body, from slices of large structures like the heart, to the three-dimensional structures of large molecules in the body. Anatomists take two general approaches to the study of the body's structures: regional and systemic. Regional anatomy is the study of the interrelationships of all of the structures in a specific body region, such as the abdomen. Studying regional anatomy helps us appreciate the interrelationships of body structures, such as how muscles, nerves, blood vessels, and other structures work together to serve a particular body region. In contrast, systemic anatomy is the study of the structures that make up a discrete body system—that is, a group of structures that work together to perform a unique body function. For example, a systemic anatomical study of the muscular system would consider all of the skeletal muscles of the body. Whereas anatomy is about structure, physiology is about function. Human physiology is the scientific study of the chemistry and physics of the structures of the body and the ways in which they work together to support the functions of life. Much of the study of physiology centers on the body's tendency toward homeostasis.

[3] (p.163) The Tissue Level of Organization > Muscle Tissue and Motion
---
Other movements are involuntary, meaning they are not under conscious control, such as the contraction of your pupil in bright light. Muscle tissue is classified into three types according to structure and function: skeletal, cardiac, and smooth. Skeletal muscle has long cylindrical fibers, is striated, and has many peripherally located nuclei. Skeletal muscle functions in voluntary movement, produces heat, and protects organs. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and around entrance points to body (e.g., mouth, anus). Cardiac muscle has short, branched fibers, is striated, and has a single central nucleus. Cardiac muscle contracts to pump blood and is located in the heart. Smooth muscle has short, spindle-shaped fibers, has no evident striation, and has a single nucleus in each fiber. Smooth muscle functions in involuntary movement, moves food, involuntary control of respiration, moves secretions, and regulates flow of blood in arteries by contraction. Smooth muscle is located in walls of major organs and passageways. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and its contraction makes possible locomotion, facial expressions, posture, and other voluntary movements of the body. Forty percent of your body mass is made up of skeletal muscle.

Dissection is still used in medical schools, anatomy courses, and in pathology labs. In order to observe structures in living people, however, a number of imaging techniques have been developed.

===

[24] (p.24, score=0.99) An Introduction to the Human Body > Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
---
These techniques allow clinicians to visualize structures inside the living body such as a cancerous tumor or a fractured bone. Gross anatomy is the study of the larger structures of the body, those visible without the aid of magnification. Macro- means "large," thus, gross anatomy is also referred to as macroscopic anatomy. In contrast, micro- means "small," and microscopic anatomy is the study of structures that can be observed only with the use of a microscope or other magnification devices. Microscopic anatomy includes cytology, the study of cells and histology, the study of tissues. As the technology of microscopes has advanced, anatomists have been able to observe smaller and smaller structures of the body, from slices of large structures like the heart, to the three-dimensional structures of large molecules in the body. Anatomists take two general approaches to the study of the body's structures: regional and systemic. Regional anatomy is the study of the interrelationships of all of the structures in a specific body region, such as the abdomen. Studying regional anatomy helps us appreciate the interrelationships of body structures, such as how muscles, nerves, blood vessels, and other structures work together to serve a particular body region. In contrast, systemic anatomy is the study of the structures that make up a discrete body system—that is, a group of structures that work together to perform a unique body function. For example, a systemic anatomical study of the muscular system would consider all of the skeletal muscles of the body. Whereas anatomy is about structure, physiology is about function. Human physiology is the scientific study of the chemistry and physics of the structures of the body and the ways in which they work together to support the functions of life. Much of the study of physiology centers on the body's tendency toward homeostasis.

===

[1] Muscle Tissue and Motion
They are contractile, meaning they can shorten and generate a pulling force. When attached between two movable objects, in other words, bones, contractions of the muscles cause the bones to move. Some muscle movement is voluntary, which means it is under conscious control.

Other movements are involuntary, meaning they are not under conscious control, such as the contraction of your pupil in bright light. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and around entrance points to body (e.g., mouth, anus). Cardiac muscle has short, branched fibers, is striated, and has a single central nucleus. Cardiac muscle contracts to pump blood and is located in the heart. Smooth muscle has short, spindle-shaped fibers, has no evident striation, and has a single nucleus in each fiber. Smooth muscle functions in involuntary movement, moves food, involuntary control of respiration, moves secretions, and regulates flow of blood in arteries by contraction. Smooth muscle is located in walls of major organs and passageways. [22] Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
When a body is dissected, its structures are cut apart in order to observe their physical attributes and their relationships to one another. Dissection is still used in medical schools, anatomy courses, and in pathology labs. In order to observe structures in living people, however, a number of imaging techniques have been developed.

[23] Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
The word "anatomy" comes from a Greek root that means "to cut apart." Human anatomy was first studied by observing the exterior of the body and observing the wounds of soldiers and other injuries. Later, physicians were allowed to dissect bodies of the dead to augment their knowledge. When a body is dissected, its structures are cut apart in order to observe their physical attributes and their relationships to one another. Dissection is still used in medical schools, anatomy courses, and in pathology labs. In order to observe structures in living people, however, a number of imaging techniques have been developed.

[24] Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
These techniques allow clinicians to visualize structures inside the living body such as a cancerous tumor or a fractured bone. Gross anatomy is the study of the larger structures of the body, those visible without the aid of magnification. Macro- means "large," thus, gross anatomy is also referred to as macroscopic anatomy. In contrast, micro- means "small," and microscopic anatomy is the study of structures that can be observed only with the use of a microscope or other magnification devices. Microscopic anatomy includes cytology, the study of cells and histology, the study of tissues. As the technology of microscopes has advanced, anatomists have been able to observe smaller and smaller structures of the body, from slices of large structures like the heart, to the three-dimensional structures of large molecules in the body. Anatomists take two general approaches to the study of the body's structures: regional and systemic. Regional anatomy is the study of the interrelationships of all of the structures in a specific body region, such as the abdomen. Studying regional anatomy helps us appreciate the interrelationships of body structures, such as how muscles, nerves, blood vessels, and other structures work together to serve a particular body region. In contrast, systemic anatomy is the study of the structures that make up a discrete body system—that is, a group of structures that work together to perform a unique body function. For example, a systemic anatomical study of the muscular system would consider all of the skeletal muscles of the body. Whereas anatomy is about structure, physiology is about function. Human physiology is the scientific study of the chemistry and physics of the structures of the body and the ways in which they work together to support the functions of life. Much of the study of physiology centers on the body's tendency toward homeostasis.

The cells are multinucleated as a result of the fusion of the many myoblasts that fuse to form each long muscle fiber. Cardiac muscle forms the contractile walls of the heart. The cells of cardiac muscle, known as cardiomyocytes, also appear striated under the microscope. Unlike skeletal muscle fibers, cardiomyocytes are single cells typically with a single centrally located nucleus. A principal characteristic of cardiomyocytes is that they contract on their own intrinsic rhythms without any external stimulation. Cardiomyocytes attach to one another with specialized cell junctions called intercalated discs.

These techniques allow clinicians to visualize structures inside the living body such as a cancerous tumor or a fractured bone. Gross anatomy is the study of the larger structures of the body, those visible without the aid of magnification. Macro- means "large," thus, gross anatomy is also referred to as macroscopic anatomy. In contrast, micro- means "small," and microscopic anatomy is the study of structures that can be observed only with the use of a microscope or other magnification devices. Microscopic anatomy includes cytology, the study of cells and histology, the study of tissues. As the technology of microscopes has advanced, anatomists have been able to observe smaller and smaller structures of the body, from slices of large structures like the heart, to the three-dimensional structures of large molecules in the body. Anatomists take two general approaches to the study of the body's structures: regional and systemic. Regional anatomy is the study of the interrelationships of all of the structures in a specific body region, such as the abdomen. Studying regional anatomy helps us appreciate the interrelationships of body structures, such as how muscles, nerves, blood vessels, and other structures work together to serve a particular body region. In contrast, systemic anatomy is the study of the structures that make up a discrete body system—that is, a group of structures that work together to perform a unique body function. For example, a systemic anatomical study of the muscular system would consider all of the skeletal muscles of the body. Whereas anatomy is about structure, physiology is about function. Human physiology is the scientific study of the chemistry and physics of the structures of the body and the ways in which they work together to support the functions of life. Much of the study of physiology centers on the body's tendency toward homeostasis.

Smooth muscle functions in involuntary movement, moves food, involuntary control of respiration, moves secretions, and regulates flow of blood in arteries by contraction. Smooth muscle is located in walls of major organs and passageways. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and its contraction makes possible locomotion, facial expressions, posture, and other voluntary movements of the body. Forty percent of your body mass is made up of skeletal muscle.

===

[17] (p.163) The Tissue Level of Organization > Muscle Tissue and Motion
---
Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and its contraction makes possible locomotion, facial expressions, posture, and other voluntary movements of the body. Forty percent of your body mass is made up of skeletal muscle.

---
Without mineral crystals, bones would flex and provide little support. Osteocytes, bone cells like chondrocytes, are located within lacunae. The histology of transverse tissue from long bone shows a typical arrangement of osteocytes in concentric circles around a central canal. Bone is a highly vascularized tissue.

[19] (p.161) The Tissue Level of Organization > Connective Tissue Supports and Protects > Supportive Connective Tissues
---

Without mineral crystals, bones would flex and provide little support. Osteocytes, bone cells like chondrocytes, are located within lacunae. The histology of transverse tissue from long bone shows a typical arrangement of osteocytes in concentric circles around a central canal. Bone is a highly vascularized tissue.

Unlike cartilage, bone tissue can recover from injuries in a relatively short time. Cancellous bone looks like a sponge under the microscope and contains empty spaces between trabeculae, or arches of bone proper. It is lighter than compact bone and found in the interior of some bones and at the end of long bones.

[22] (p.24) An Introduction to the Human Body > Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
---
When a body is dissected, its structures are cut apart in order to observe their physical attributes and their relationships to one another. Dissection is still used in medical schools, anatomy courses, and in pathology labs. In order to observe structures in living people, however, a number of imaging techniques have been developed.

===

[23] (p.24) An Introduction to the Human Body > Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
---
The word "anatomy" comes from a Greek root that means "to cut apart." Human anatomy was first studied by observing the exterior of the body and observing the wounds of soldiers and other injuries. Later, physicians were allowed to dissect bodies of the dead to augment their knowledge. When a body is dissected, its structures are cut apart in order to observe their physical attributes and their relationships to one another. Dissection is still used in medical schools, anatomy courses, and in pathology labs. In order to observe structures in living people, however, a number of imaging techniques have been developed.

===

[24] (p.24, score=0.99) An Introduction to the Human Body > Overview of Anatomy and Physiology
---
These techniques allow clinicians to visualize structures inside the living body such as a cancerous tumor or a fractured bone. Gross anatomy is the study of the larger structures of the body, those visible without the aid of magnification. Macro- means "large," thus, gross anatomy is also referred to as macroscopic anatomy. In contrast, micro- means "small," and microscopic anatomy is the study of structures that can be observed only with the use of a microscope or other magnification devices. Microscopic anatomy includes cytology, the study of cells and histology, the study of tissues. As the technology of microscopes has advanced, anatomists have been able to observe smaller and smaller structures of the body, from slices of large structures like the heart, to the three-dimensional structures of large molecules in the body. Anatomists take two general approaches to the study of the body's structures: regional and systemic. Regional anatomy is the study of the interrelationships of all of the structures in a specific body region, such as the abdomen. Studying regional anatomy helps us appreciate the interrelationships of body structures, such as how muscles, nerves, blood vessels, and other structures work together to serve a particular body region. In contrast, systemic anatomy is the study of the structures that make up a discrete body system—that is, a group of structures that work together to perform a unique body function. For example, a systemic anatomical study of the muscular system would consider all of the skeletal muscles of the body. Whereas anatomy is about structure, physiology is about function. Human physiology is the scientific study of the chemistry and physics of the structures of the body and the ways in which they work together to support the functions of life. Much of the study of physiology centers on the body's tendency toward homeostasis.

===

[1] Muscle Tissue and Motion
They are contractile, meaning they can shorten and generate a pulling force. When attached between two movable objects, in other words, bones, contractions of the muscles cause the bones to move. Some muscle movement is voluntary, which means it is under conscious control.

[2] Muscle Tissue and Motion
Cancellous bone looks like a sponge under the microscope and contains empty spaces between trabeculae, or arches of bone proper. It is lighter than compact bone and found in the interior of some bones and at the end of long bones. Muscles are contractile, meaning they can shorten and generate a pulling force. When attached between two movable objects, in other words, bones, contractions of the muscles cause the bones to move. Some muscle movement is voluntary, which means it is under conscious control.

[3] Muscle Tissue and Motion
Other movements are involuntary, meaning they are not under conscious control, such as the contraction of your pupil in bright light. Muscle tissue is classified into three types according to structure and function: skeletal, cardiac, and smooth. Skeletal muscle has long cylindrical fibers, is striated, and has many peripherally located nuclei. Skeletal muscle functions in voluntary movement, produces heat, and protects organs. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and around entrance points to body (e.g., mouth, anus). Cardiac muscle has short, branched fibers, is striated, and has a single central nucleus. Cardiac muscle contracts to pump blood and is located in the heart. Smooth muscle has short, spindle-shaped fibers, has no evident striation, and has a single nucleus in each fiber. Smooth muscle functions in involuntary movement, moves food, involuntary control of respiration, moves secretions, and regulates flow of blood in arteries by contraction. Smooth muscle is located in walls of major organs and passageways. Skeletal muscle is attached to bones and its contraction makes possible locomotion, facial expressions, posture, and other voluntary movements of the body. Forty percent of your body mass is made up of skeletal muscle.
